mappings.rationaleAI: Natural law theory, legal positivism, Dworkin's rights-based theory, and Shapiro's planning theory directly address problems of the philosophy of law (A.1.b). The relationship between law, morality, and justice covers the influence of natural law (A.1.c). Historical survey from foundational theorists addresses the scope of Western philosophy of law (A.1.a) and its historical development (A.1.d).

Criminal law, civil law, constitutional law, and the role of courts, judges, and lawyers address modern systems of law (B.4) and the profession of law (D, D.1). Institutional focus on democratic governance relates to Anglo-American common law (B.4.a).

Dicey's theorization and successors including Hayek, Fuller, Dworkin, and Bingham address approaches to jurisprudence (A.1.b). Due process and legality connect to law and morality (A.1.c). Classical and early modern sources address historical survey of legal theories (A.1.d).

Systematic comparison of legal systems including common law and civil law addresses Anglo-American common law (B.4.a) and continental civil law (B.4.b). Assessing legal differences across jurisdictions is the study of parallels among diverse legal systems (C).

Islamic jurisprudence as a major legal system directly addresses non-Western philosophies of law, specifically Islamic philosophy (A.2). Application across the Middle East, Asia, and Africa addresses modern systems of law (B.4).
Philosophy in Islamic lands covering reason, religious belief, ethics, and political philosophy provides intellectual context for Islamic legal philosophy (A.2).

Confucian influence on state ideology and governance provides foundations for Chinese philosophy of law (A.2). Impact on governance and social order in Chinese civilization connects to Chinese law (B.2.c).

Evolution of American law covering common law traditions and constitutional development addresses Anglo-American common law (B.4.a). Court systems and legal education connect to the profession of law (D.1).
Ancient Egyptian kingship, customs, ethics, and social organization provide context for Egyptian law (B.2.a).
Mesopotamian civilizations covering codified law, kingship, and bureaucracy directly address cuneiform law (B.2.b).
Political concepts from Greek city-states including democracy, oligarchy, and tyranny provide the institutional context for Greek law (B.2.d).
Multi-ethnic governance, federal government, and administrative systems across Hellenistic successor kingdoms address Hellenistic law (B.2.e).
Political structures including the cursus honorum, Senate, and magistracies provide the institutional foundation for Roman law (B.2.f).
Imperial administration, provincial governance, and the Senate address Roman law (B.2.f) in its mature imperial phase.
European history 500-1500 CE covering transition from Roman provinces to barbarian kingdoms addresses medieval European law (B.3) and origins of medieval European law (B.3.a).

Magna Carta's provisions limiting royal authority address origins of medieval European law (B.3.a). Baronial reform connects to medieval constitutional law (B.3.b). Property and feudal provisions relate to private law in medieval Europe (B.3.c).

Aquinas's natural law theory addresses law and morality (A.1.c). As a 13th-century theologian, he contributes to historical survey of legal theories (A.1.d). His Catholic scholasticism connects to development of canon law (B.3.d).
Consequentialism, deontology, and virtue ethics address the relationship between law and morality (A.1.c).
Stoic ethics concerning virtue and natural law influenced Roman legal philosophy, addressing law and morality (A.1.c) and contributing to historical survey of legal theories (A.1.d).
Leviathan's social contract theory addresses approaches to jurisprudence (A.1.b). Hobbes contributes to historical survey of legal theories (A.1.d).

Locke's defense of toleration and responsible government addresses philosophy of law (A.1.b). Natural rights connect to law and morality (A.1.c). As an Enlightenment thinker, he contributes to historical survey (A.1.d).
Avicenna's innovations in logic, metaphysics, and his legacy in both the Islamic world and Latin Christendom provide intellectual foundations for non-Western philosophies of law (A.2).

Medieval Christian, Muslim, and Jewish thinkers including political philosophy address historical survey of legal theories (A.1.d). Muslim contributions connect to non-Western philosophies of law (A.2).

Medieval English common law origins address medieval constitutional law (B.3.b). Anglo-American legal tradition connects to common law (B.4.a). Safeguard against detention relates to the practice of law (D).

The jury system from ancient Athens through medieval England addresses Anglo-American common law (B.4.a). Mixed panels address continental civil law (B.4.b). Comparative analysis addresses parallels among diverse legal systems (C). Trial processes relate to the profession of law (D, D.1).
Natural rights philosophy through the Universal Declaration of Human Rights addresses the relationship between law and morality (A.1.c).
Theory and practice of legal interpretation addresses approaches to jurisprudence (A.1.b) through hermeneutic methodology.
Separation of church and state addresses the relationship between law and morality (A.1.c) by examining how legal systems disentangled from religious moral authority.
